items.title: Trump signs executive order on AI innovation and security
items.summary: President Trump signed an executive order directing federal agencies to build a framework for secure deployment of advanced AI models.
items.details: On June 2, 2026, the order directed agencies to strengthen cyber defenses and create a voluntary process for developers to give the government early access to frontier AI models for up to 30 days before release. It also directs agencies to accelerate hiring of cybersecurity specialists.

items.title: Executive order moves about 8,000 federal positions to Schedule Policy/Career
items.summary: An executive order formalized a new federal employment category reclassifying roughly 8,000 senior policy-influencing positions.
items.details: Signed June 3, 2026, the order reclassifies about 8,000 senior policy-influencing positions into Schedule Policy/Career and makes them at-will. The administration said it aims to improve accountability; federal unions and employee organizations criticized the change.

items.title: State Department to add premium expedited visa service
items.summary: The State Department announced a premium expedited service for foreign business and tourist visa applicants.
items.details: Reported June 9, 2026, the optional service would cost applicants $750 on top of the initial $185 fee for expedited processing of business or tourist visas.

items.title: State Department approves possible counter-drone sale to Kuwait
items.summary: The U.S. State Department approved a possible sale of nearly $2 billion in counter-unmanned aerial systems to Kuwait.
items.details: Announced in a Friday release reported June 9, 2026, Kuwait requested the counter-UAS platforms, built by Anduril, to improve its ability to counter current and future threats.

items.title: Secretary Rubio holds calls with EU and Mexican officials
items.summary: Secretary of State Marco Rubio held separate calls with the European Commission president and Mexico's foreign secretary.
items.details: On June 9, 2026, the State Department issued readouts of Secretary Rubio's calls with European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen and Mexican Foreign Secretary Velasco.

items.title: Continued U.S.-Iran exchanges reported
items.summary: U.S. Central Command and Iran exchanged military actions, and the Treasury imposed additional sanctions on Iranian networks.
items.details: Reported in early June 2026, the U.S. military struck a drone ground control site after Iran launched drones at ships in the strait, and Iran fired a ballistic missile at Kuwait that was intercepted. The Treasury sanctioned Iranian finance and weapons procurement networks.

items.title: Senate passes S. 2, the Secure America Act
items.summary: The U.S. Senate passed S. 2 on a simple-majority vote.
items.details: The Senate voted on June 5, 2026 to pass S. 2, the Secure America Act. The measure required a simple majority and passed; the recorded yea votes represented about 47% of the country's population by GovTrack's apportionment.

items.title: House passes fraud-prevention bills under suspension
items.summary: The House passed several fraud-prevention measures, including the TRUE Accountability Act and a workforce training bill.
items.details: On June 8, 2026, the House passed H.R. 8466, the TRUE Accountability Act, 384-0, and H.R. 8428, the Federal Fraud Prevention Workforce Training Act, 393-0, both under suspension of the rules during what was described as 'Fraud Week.'

items.title: House considers Treasury fraud and payment bills
items.summary: The House took up bills to establish fraud-prevention functions at Treasury and to authorize pausing and segmenting payments.
items.details: On June 9, 2026, the House provided for consideration of H.R. 8312, to establish fraud prevention and data-sharing authorities and a permanent Inspector General for Fraud, Accountability, and Recovery at Treasury, and H.R. 8464, to authorize pausing and segmenting payments.

items.title: Supreme Court permits Alabama to use challenged congressional map
items.summary: The Supreme Court cleared the way for Alabama to use a congressional map in the 2026 elections that lower courts had found racially discriminatory.
items.details: In an unsigned order issued June 2, 2026, the Court overturned a three-judge panel and allowed the 2023 map. The three liberal justices dissented; Justice Sotomayor wrote the dissent, joined by Justices Kagan and Jackson.

items.title: Supreme Court issues June opinions in pharmaceutical and FCC cases
items.summary: The Supreme Court released several opinions on June 4, 2026, including Hikma Pharmaceuticals v. Amarin Pharma and FCC v. AT&T.
items.details: Among the June 4, 2026 decisions, Hikma v. Amarin concerned a drug containing icosapent ethyl, and FCC v. AT&T involved an FCC investigation into alleged mishandling of customer location data by cellular service providers.

items.title: Major Supreme Court rulings still pending before recess
items.summary: The Court has several major cases left to decide, including on birthright citizenship, campaign finance, and mail-in ballots.
items.details: As of June 4, 2026, pending cases include the administration's effort to end birthright citizenship, a campaign finance case on party-candidate coordination, transgender sports laws in West Virginia and Idaho, and a Mississippi mail-ballot deadline case.

items.title: California and Nevada hold 2026 primaries
items.summary: California held its primary on June 2 and Nevada held its primary on June 9, with results being counted.
items.details: California's June 2, 2026 primary results are being canvassed, with certification due July 10. Nevada's June 9, 2026 primary included contested governor and U.S. House races; the Associated Press provides results data to news organizations.
items.source: California Secretary of State / The Washington Post

items.title: Redistricting battles continue ahead of midterms
items.summary: Following recent court rulings, multiple states are weighing or finalizing new congressional maps for the 2026 and 2028 cycles.
items.details: Reported June 5, 2026, the Supreme Court cleared Alabama's preferred map, while Georgia's governor called a special session for a 2028 map and New York lawmakers advanced legislation toward new lines. California's voter-approved map is being used for 2026.

items.title: Brennan Center tallies 2026 state voting law changes
items.summary: At least nine states enacted 12 restrictive voting laws and at least six states enacted 16 expansive voting laws in early 2026.
items.details: As of a May 2026 roundup, South Dakota and Utah now require documentary proof of citizenship from all registrants, while Florida, Kentucky, and Mississippi require such documents from some voters. New Hampshire increased required polling stations per registered voters.

AL.title: Supreme Court allows Alabama's 2023 congressional map for 2026
AL.summary: Alabama will use a congressional map favored by Republicans in the 2026 midterms after a Supreme Court order.
AL.details: On June 2, 2026, the Supreme Court overturned a lower-court panel and allowed Alabama's 2023 map, meaning the state's 2026 elections will feature six Republican-leaning districts and one Democratic-leaning district. The Court's three liberal justices dissented.

CA.title: California certifies count of June 2 primary
CA.summary: California held its statewide primary on June 2, 2026, with results being tallied during the canvass period.
CA.details: Counties must report final official results to the Secretary of State by July 3, 2026, with certification set for July 10. The primary covered governor, other statewide offices, U.S. House, and legislative contests.

NV.title: Nevada holds June 9 primary
NV.summary: Nevada voters cast ballots in primary contests for governor and U.S. House on June 9, 2026.
NV.details: Results were being counted June 10, 2026, in races that included crowded Republican and Democratic primaries. David Flippo was projected to win the Republican primary for Nevada's 2nd Congressional District.

IA.title: Iowa governor signs final bills of 2026 session
IA.summary: Governor Kim Reynolds signed the final bills from the 2026 legislative session on June 2, 2026.
IA.details: Among the measures, House File 2694 bars a governor from restricting private businesses or ordering places of worship to close during emergencies and requires legislative approval for changes to election laws. Senate File 2218 adds work-authorization checks for educator licenses.

LA.title: Louisiana governor signs marijuana penalty bill
LA.summary: Governor Jeff Landry signed HB 568, increasing penalties for marijuana use near schools and universities.
LA.details: Signed and reported June 4, 2026, the law makes smoking or vaping cannabis on campus or within 2,000 feet of school property a felony punishable by up to one year in jail and a $1,000 fine. It takes effect August 1.

CT.title: Connecticut governor signs solar energy bill
CT.summary: Governor Ned Lamont signed HB 5340, addressing solar power incentives and permitting.
CT.details: Signed June 4, 2026, the legislation extends incentives, streamlines permitting, and advances balcony solar in Connecticut.

MD.title: Maryland enacts state Voting Rights Act provision
MD.summary: Maryland enacted Senate Bill 255, a component of the Maryland Voting Rights Act.
MD.details: Reported June 2, 2026, SB 255 establishes protections for historically disenfranchised voters and bans discriminatory voting systems as part of the Maryland Voting Rights Act.

GA.title: Georgia governor calls special session on redistricting
GA.summary: Governor Brian Kemp called for a special legislative session to take up a new congressional map for the 2028 cycle.
GA.details: Reported June 5, 2026, the special session is scheduled for later in the month. Mississippi Republicans are also expected to take up redistricting.

SC.title: South Carolina holds primary elections
SC.summary: South Carolina voters cast ballots in 2026 primary contests, including U.S. Senate.
SC.details: Results from South Carolina's 2026 primaries, including U.S. Senate and U.S. House races, were being reported on June 9-10, 2026.

disclaimer: This digest reports verifiable recent developments from cited sources and takes no position for or against any party, official, or policy.
